summary: The diff adds a minimal Harness CLI (validate/advance), static contract tests, and adapter wrapper hardening (resolved claude executable, stdin prompt, system-prompt/json-schema flags, structured_output preference, BOM-tolerant payload loading). Changes are largely backward-compatible and well-tested. One defense-in-depth regression stands out: the Claude permission mode changed from `plan` (read-only enforced at CLI level) to `default`, relying on `--tools ""` and prompt instructions to enforce read-only behavior. Several minor robustness gaps (null structured_output, hardcoded budget, permissive evidence-path checks) are noted but not blocking. Real-wrapper smoke and unit suite (33 tests) pass per verification.

findings.title: Permission mode regression: `plan` -> `default` weakens read-only guarantee
findings.evidence: The adapter command list changed `--permission-mode` from `plan` to `default`. The previous mode enforced read-only behavior at the Claude CLI level; the new mode relies entirely on `--tools ""` plus a system prompt that says 'Do not modify files'. If `--tools ""` does not fully suppress every built-in tool (e.g., bash/shell on some Claude builds) or if a future change relaxes the tools list, the reviewer invocation could attempt file modifications. The original code paired the read-only system prompt with a read-only permission mode as defense in depth; that pairing is now broken. No test or comment explains why `plan` was incompatible with the new `--system-prompt`/`--json-schema` flags.
findings.recommendation: Re-test whether `--permission-mode plan` works with `--system-prompt` and `--json-schema`; if it does, revert to `plan`. If `default` is required, document why, and add a regression test asserting that `--tools` remains empty and that the resolved command cannot include write-capable tools. Consider also adding `--allowedTools` or `--disallowedTools` as an explicit allow-list instead of relying on an empty `--tools` string.

findings.title: `_extract_review_content` does not handle null/empty `structured_output`
findings.evidence: The new branch `if "structured_output" in parsed: content = parsed["structured_output"]` checks only key presence. If Claude emits `{"structured_output": null}` or `{"structured_output": ""}` alongside a valid `result`, the function will set `content = None`/empty and never fall through to the `result` branch, which can produce a downstream schema error or empty review. No test covers this null/empty case.
findings.recommendation: Guard the branch, e.g. `if parsed.get("structured_output"): content = parsed["structured_output"]`, and add a unit test where `structured_output` is null/empty but `result` contains valid JSON.

findings.title: `--max-budget-usd` hardcoded to 1 with no payload override
findings.evidence: The command list hardcodes `"--max-budget-usd", "1"`. Other parameters (timeout, prompt contents) are payload-driven, but the cost ceiling is not. A larger diff or stricter review cannot raise the budget without code changes, and an operator cannot cap it lower for smoke runs.
findings.recommendation: Read the budget from `payload.get("max_budget_usd", 1)` (validated to be a positive number) so it is configurable per invocation while preserving the safe default.

findings.title: Evidence path validation accepts absolute paths and does not confine to run directory
findings.evidence: `validate_evidence_paths` checks `evidence_path.is_absolute()` and only verifies existence; relative paths are checked under `root` and `run_dir` but are not normalized, so `../../etc/passwd`-style relative paths would be resolved and accepted if the file happens to exist. This is a validation-only helper (no content is read), so impact is limited, but it can mask evidence that points outside the intended run tree.
findings.recommendation: Resolve evidence paths with `(run_dir / raw_path).resolve()` and assert the resolved path is within `run_dir.resolve()` (and/or `root.resolve()`) before accepting. At minimum, document the intended scope.

findings.title: Static-contract test loads adapter as a second module instance
findings.evidence: `test_static_contracts.py` uses `importlib.util.spec_from_file_location("claude_review_adapter", ADAPTER_PATH)` and registers it in `sys.modules` under a name distinct from the `adapter` module used by `test_claude_review_adapter.py`. Constants like `TERMINAL_STATUSES` are therefore evaluated against a separately loaded copy. If module-level state or imported dependencies diverge between the two load paths, drift could pass here while failing in the other test file (or vice versa).
findings.recommendation: Import the adapter through the same path the wrapper uses (e.g., add `mcp/claude-review` to sys.path and `import adapter`), or factor the contract assertions into a shared helper that both test files consume, so there is a single source of truth for the loaded module.

findings.title: Local `pip install` mutated environment without a root lockfile
findings.evidence: Verification notes: 'The local `python -m pip install` changed the current Python environment but did not add a root dependency lockfile.' Reproducing the run on another machine or in CI may install different versions of `mcp` and transitive deps, which could affect both server startup and adapter parsing of Claude output.
findings.recommendation: Commit a lockfile (e.g., `pip-compile` output or `uv.lock`) for `mcp/claude-review/requirements.txt` so the MCP server runtime is reproducible.

tested: Resolved `claude` executable is used as command[0] (test_run_uses_resolved_claude_executable)
Prompt is passed via stdin (`input=`) and NOT embedded in argv; TASK_CONTENT asserted in kwargs['input'] and absent from command join
`--system-prompt`, `--json-schema`, and `--permission-mode default` are present in the constructed command
`structured_output` is preferred over `result` when both are present (test_normalize_prefers_structured_output)
UTF-8 BOM-prefixed payload input loads correctly (test_wrapper_load_payload_accepts_utf8_bom)
CLI validate accepts example-fast-doc-change run and BOM-prefixed state.json
CLI validate rejects missing evidence paths
CLI advance permits codex normal transitions and refreshes updated_at
CLI advance does not write candidate state when candidate validation fails (atomicity)
CLI advance rejects invalid transitions and non-codex actors
Static contracts: workflow registry, status enum, and review-status mapping match docs and schema
Real Claude wrapper smoke produced terminal findings and wrote output/review/raw-log files
MCP server import and startup smoke after requirements install
not_tested: Long-lived MCP client session through Codex's MCP runtime (explicitly noted in verification)
Cloud or CI environment behavior (explicitly noted in verification)
Whether `--permission-mode plan` is actually incompatible with the new `--system-prompt`/`--json-schema` flags (no A/B test in diff)
Behavior when Claude emits `structured_output: null` alongside a valid `result`
Real Claude review of this exact Standard diff (referenced in plan but output not embedded in the review target)
Concurrent or repeated CLI advance calls (no locking test)
Evidence path traversal (e.g., `../` escapes) — no test asserts rejection
Behavior on non-Windows hosts when `shutil.which('claude')` resolves a shell script or symlink chain
residual_risks: Switching `--permission-mode` from `plan` to `default` shifts the read-only guarantee from enforced to advisory; correctness depends on `--tools ""` fully suppressing all tools across Claude CLI versions.
Claude output shape may vary across model versions; even with `--json-schema`, schema-constrained parsing could fall back to `result` strings that need tolerant normalization.
Hardcoded `--max-budget-usd 1` may truncate large reviews or reject long diffs without a clear terminal status if Claude aborts mid-run.
No dependency lockfile means reproducibility of the MCP server runtime is not guaranteed across machines.

The CLI's state machine is duplicated in spirit by `harness/core/state-authority.md` and `state.schema.json`; future drift between these three sources is only caught if static-contract tests are kept in sync.
Evidence path validation is existence-only and path-confinement-agnostic, so a malformed run could pass validation while pointing at unrelated files.
